title: Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum
taxonomy: Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum is classified as a species of grass in the family Poaceae. It belongs to the genus Muhlenbergia and is further categorized into the subfamily Festucoideae. This classification places Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum within the order Poales. As a member of the family Poaceae, it shares characteristics with other grasses, such as monocotyledonous leaves and a flowering structure composed of spikelets. Further classification details include its ranking as a species within the kingdom Plantae and the phylum Magnoliophyta.
uses: Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum, commonly known as the short-misted or short-spiked beard grass, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's roots and leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of arthritis and other inflammatory conditions. Additionally, Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum is believed to have antimicrobial properties, making it a potential treatment for wounds and skin infections. However, the effectiveness and safety of these uses have not been extensively scientifically studied or verified. Further research is needed to confirm the medicinal potential of Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum.

intro: Muhlenbergia brachyelytrum, also known as short-hairy muhly or short-awned muhly, is a species of grass native to North America. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help to reduce fever, alleviate pain, and protect against oxidative stress.
